---
title: Template Everything
---

*Calepin* is built on one idea: **templates everywhere**. Every piece of output, from a single callout box to a full website, is controlled by a template you can read, copy, and customize. No Rust, no Lua, no Python, no shell scripts. Just text files with variable placeholders.

Learn one syntax and you can change how *Calepin* renders anything.


# Five layers of templates

*Calepin* uses templates at five levels, from smallest to largest scope. Each level uses the same Jinja syntax, so what you learn for one applies to all.

## 1. Shortcuts

Shortcuts are Jinja expressions you write directly in your document body. They insert content, call functions, or use control flow, without touching any external files. With shortcuts, you can:

- Insert the document title, author, date, or any front matter field into your prose.
- Access custom variables defined in the YAML preamble.
- Read system environment variables.
- Insert a page break that produces the right output for every format.
- Embed a YouTube or Vimeo video.
- Render keyboard shortcuts with proper markup.
- Generate placeholder text or images for layout prototyping.
- Show or hide paragraphs based on the output format.
- Loop over lists of items from your variables.
- Include the contents of another file.
- Call custom functions provided by plugins.

See [Shortcuts](shortcuts.html) for the full reference.


## 2. Elements

Every rendered element (code blocks, figures, fenced divs, bracketed spans, theorems, callouts) passes through an element template. These are small files that control the markup for a single component.

To customize how any element renders, drop a template file in your project directory. For instance, you can restyle callout boxes, change how theorems are numbered, or create entirely new div types with custom attributes. See [Elements](elements.html) for all available variables and naming conventions.


## 3. Pages

The page template wraps the entire rendered body in a document shell: the HTML skeleton, the LaTeX preamble, or the Typst equivalent.

To use a custom layout, drop a template file in your project directory with placeholders for the title, stylesheet, and body. This is how you create blog layouts, slide shells, dashboard wrappers, or branded reports without writing code. See [Pages](pages.html) for all page template variables.


## 4. Collections

*Calepin* can render a collection of documents into a structured website with navigation, search, and a consistent layout. The collection system has its own set of templates for the navbar, sidebar, document shell, and more.

To override any collection template, create files in `templates/website/` at your project root. See [Collections](sites.html) for configuration and customization.


## 5. Preamble

The YAML front matter at the top of every document controls metadata, output options, and custom variables. For collections, a project-level configuration file provides defaults shared across all documents. See [YAML](yaml.html) for all supported fields.
